skill.name: vercel-deploy
skill.description: Deploy applications and websites to Vercel preview deployments through a shell script that packages a project and uploads it to a hosted deployment endpoint.
skill.summary: Package a local web project and create a claimable Vercel preview deployment.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static shell and network findings are partly true positives because the script creates an archive and uploads it to a hardcoded hosted endpoint. Many SKILL.md backtick, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance flags are false positives from documentation text, but the semantic risk remains high because project source and possible secrets can be sent outside the local workspace.

high_findings.title: Project Archive Uploaded to Hardcoded External Endpoint
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: the script defines a hosted deploy endpoint, archives the selected project, and uploads that archive with curl. This can disclose source code, configuration files, and local secrets to a third-party service.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint, archive creation, and upload are directly present in the script. Deployment requires network upload, but the data exposure risk is clear and material.

medium_findings.title: Deployment Package Does Not Exclude Common Secret Files
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: the tar command excludes node_modules and .git only. Files such as .env, private keys, build artifacts, and service credentials can be included if they are present in the project directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exclusion list is visible and limited. I did not find evidence of secret filtering before upload.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Directory or Tarball Is Uploaded Without Review
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: the script accepts a path argument or an existing .tgz file and uploads it to the deployment endpoint. There is no manifest preview, size warning, or confirmation step before data leaves the workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path handling and upload are explicit. The risk depends on user choice, but the script provides no guardrail against uploading sensitive archives.

low_findings.title: Script Can Rename a User HTML File
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: for static HTML projects, the script may rename a single HTML file to index.html. This local mutation is intentional but can surprise users and alter their working tree.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The rename branch is directly present and uses mv. The behavior is limited to one static HTML case, so severity is low.

low_findings.title: Documentation Backtick and Weak-Crypto Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: SKILL.md contains fenced examples, inline code, URLs, and framework text. I found no prompt injection attempt, cryptographic operation, or executable backtick expansion in the Markdown content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited Markdown lines are descriptive text and examples. There is no evidence that these lines execute commands or define weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Substitutions Are Mostly Legitimate Workflow Steps
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE WITH RESIDUAL RISK: command substitutions gather file paths, parse responses, and detect frameworks. They are not direct command injection evidence because variables are quoted, but they support the higher-risk archive upload workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell substitutions are visible and appear quoted in the relevant command contexts. The residual concern is data handling, not direct shell injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded Network Upload Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The deploy endpoint is hardcoded and receives the generated project archive through curl. Users cannot easily verify ownership or storage behavior from this skill alone.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and upload command are explicit. The risk is semantic rather than speculative because source archives are transmitted.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Project Packaging
dangerous_patterns.description: The tar command packages the project while excluding only node_modules and .git. This can include files that developers do not intend to deploy.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The archive command and exclusion list are directly visible. No additional filtering is shown in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Mutation During Deployment Preparation
dangerous_patterns.description: The script can rename a single root HTML file to index.html before packaging. This changes user files as a side effect of deployment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The mv command is gated by static HTML detection. The behavior is clear and narrow.

content.user_title: Deploy Projects to Vercel Preview URLs
content.value_statement: Publishing a local web project for review can be slow when the user has no deployment setup. This skill packages the project, sends it to a hosted deploy endpoint, and returns preview and claim links.
actual_capabilities: Runs a Bash deployment script from the skill files.
Accepts a project directory or an existing .tgz archive.
Detects many JavaScript frameworks from package.json dependencies.
Creates a compressed project archive while excluding node_modules and .git.
Uploads the archive to a hosted deployment endpoint.
Returns a preview URL and a claim URL when deployment succeeds.
limitations: It requires outbound network access to the deployment endpoint.
It can upload local source files and secrets if they are inside the selected path.
It does not authenticate with a user's Vercel account before upload.
It may rename a single root HTML file to index.html for static projects.

use_cases.title: Share a quick frontend preview
use_cases.description: Create a temporary preview link for a local web project before setting up a full deployment pipeline.

use_cases.title: Review generated site output
use_cases.description: Deploy a generated static site and inspect the live result through a preview URL.

use_cases.title: Hand off a claimable deployment
use_cases.description: Generate a deployment that another person can claim in a Vercel account through the returned claim link.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy current project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple preview deployment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy the current project with the Vercel deploy skill and show me the preview link.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy a specific folder
prompt_templates.scenario: User knows which project directory should be published.
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy the web app in ./apps/site and return both the preview URL and claim URL.

prompt_templates.title: Check before deploying
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants a safer deployment workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the project folder for likely secrets, then deploy it only if no sensitive files are included.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy and summarize result
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with deployment context and follow-up reporting.
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy ./dist as a static site, identify any framework detection result, and summarize the deployment links and any warnings.

output_examples.output: The skill prepares the project, detects the framework if package metadata exists, uploads the archive, and reports the live preview and claim links.
The skill checks for a single root HTML file, may rename it to index.html, creates a deployment package, and returns the preview result.
The skill uses the provided archive directly, skips framework detection, uploads it to the deploy endpoint, and reports the deployment links.

best_practices: Inspect the selected project path before deployment.
Remove .env files, keys, tokens, and private data before running the skill.
Use the returned preview URL for review before sharing the claim URL.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy a whole home directory or broad workspace path.
Do not include private environment files or credentials in the project folder.
Do not treat the preview URL as a private or access-controlled deployment.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Vercel login?
faq.answer: No. The skill describes a no-authentication flow that returns a preview URL and a claim URL.

faq.question: What project types can it detect?
faq.answer: It checks package.json for many JavaScript frameworks, including Next.js, Gatsby, Remix, Astro, SvelteKit, Nuxt, Angular, Express, and Vite.

faq.question: Can it deploy static HTML?
faq.answer: Yes. If no package.json exists, it treats the project as static HTML and may rename one root HTML file to index.html.

faq.question: What data leaves my machine?
faq.answer: The selected directory or provided archive is uploaded to the hosted deployment endpoint, except for excluded node_modules and .git folders.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated high?
faq.answer: The script can upload source code and secrets to a hardcoded external endpoint without a built-in review or confirmation step.

faq.question: What should I check before using it?
faq.answer: Check for .env files, private keys, service credentials, internal documents, and any files that should not be deployed.
